reviews.author: Amir Goldstein
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Amir Goldstein noted that if passthrough open is enabled, the server should not handle truncate and write operations, suggesting making conflicting fuse configurations mutually exclusive.

Reviewer Amir Goldstein argued that passthrough open should be considered an inode operation, similar to CREATE, because it operates on the backing inode, but acknowledged that this perspective is confusing and suggested keeping the current implementation.

Reviewer questioned whether the backing inode needs to be opened with O_TRUNC when FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_OPEN is set, pointing out that if it is, the file will be truncated and fuse will read the size attribute.

Reviewer noted that if the server doesn't see the open and doesn't provide ff->fh, then file operations carrying fh cannot be sent to the server, suggesting additional work is needed for passthrough open

Reviewer noted that the patch does not address all previously raised issues and suggested further discussion on how to resolve them without overly complicating the code.
sentiment_signals: no clear resolution of previous issues
reviews.raw_body:
I think we need to reduce complexity.
If we do open passthrough, then the server should not handle truncate.
same for write, if we passthrough write, server should not handle killpriv.
If this means that PASSTHROUGH_INO conflicts with some other
fuse configs, then I think we should make those configs mutually exclusive,
at least for the start.

---

I would argue that in this context, passthrough open *is* an inode operation,
just like CREATE, because it operates on the backing inode, not ff->passthrough.
But this POV is confusing when considering file_operations and inode_operations
so let it stay as you did.

---

Do we need to do that?
Is the backing inode being opened with O_TRUNC? maybe not?
If it does it will be truncated and fuse will read the size attribute. no?

---

I think if the server did not see the open and did not provide ff->fh
then all the file ops that carry fh cannot be sent to the server. Right?
So implementing passthrough open may need a bit more work.

---

Joanne,

I am done with this round of review.
Nice work!
Thanks for getting this out the door.
Let's see if we find a way to tackle the issues found so far
without over complicating the code.

Thanks,
Amir.

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary:
Author agrees to simplify the handling of passthrough operations by removing the FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_INODE_OPS check and instead directly setting the ops mask based on the file type.

The author acknowledged that the server typically handles the truncating logic when it handles FUSE_OPEN, and agreed to restructure the handling of FUSE_ATOMIC_O_TRUNC in v2.

Author acknowledged a potential issue with passthrough open handling in overlayfs, promising to investigate further.

Author is uncertain about reviewer's feedback regarding operations requiring filehandle (ff->fh), plans to research further before addressing the concern.

Author acknowledged that the passthrough open logic needs to be updated to handle both regular files and directories, agreed to make changes in a future version.
reviews.raw_body:
That makes a lot of sense and will make things simpler. I think that's
a great idea.

---

You're right, the truncation will already be handled by the backing
filesystem. I'll fix this in v2.

---

I'll look at the overlayfs layer and do some research on this. Thanks
for pointing this out

---

I need to research more into this but my understanding was that the
logic in the patch avoids sending any ops that require ff->fh. Though
maybe I'm missing something here...

---

Thank you for reviewing this series, Amir. All your comments were
immensely helpful.

Thanks,
Joanne

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a need to add several more operations to the passthrough open mask, promising to include them in version 2 of the patch.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a need for additional operations
reviews.raw_body:
Ahh okay I see, I'm missing FUSE_IOCTL, FUSE_LSEEK, FUSE_FLOCK and
some others. Sorry for missing that, I'll fix that up in v2.

Thanks,
Joanne

reviews.author: Amir Goldstein
reviews.summary: Reviewer Amir Goldstein suggested that FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_OPEN should imply skipping optional operations like lseek/ioctl/flock, and proposed a mental model where atomic open sends RELEASE to the server and uses FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_OPEN semantics for the first open.
reviews.raw_body:
FWIW, those are all optional operations that already have
a default kernel implementation, so I suggest that OPEN passthrough
implies that we just skip them, same as you did for FLUSH.

It is worth mentioning that the atomic open code path still goes
through the server, which means that server could return
FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_OPEN entry and either opt-in
for FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH or not and also that the server does
provide ff->fh and does expect release in this case.

Semantically this seems fine, the only thing to watch out for is
that because FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_OPEN is for the lifetime
of the inode, it is almost as if ff->passthrough_open is redundant,
only it is not, for this first atomic open.

Maybe the mental model would be simpler if atomic open
would send RELEASE to the server and use the
FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_OPEN semantics
(force FOPEN_PASSTHROUGH and no lseek/ioctl/flock) for this
first open as well, to be more consistent.

Thanks,
Amir.

2026-04-20.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module called 'passthrough open'. It allows the server to indicate that certain operations should be skipped and instead performed directly on the backing file. Specifically, when the FUSE_PASSTHROUGH_OP_OPEN flag is set, the kernel will skip sending FUSE_OPEN/FUSE_OPENDIR requests to the server and open the backing file directly. This also implies passthrough readdir for directories and read/write operations for regular files. Additionally, if the server handles truncation logic in its FUSE_OPEN handler, the vfs layer will call handle_truncate() which may trigger a fuse_setattr() call to truncate the backing file directly or send a FUSE_SETATTR request to the server.
